sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
The following limitations apply only to deployments hosted in
MongoDB Atlas. If any of these limits present a problem for your
organization, contact [Atlas support](atlas/support).

- You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/stable/command/atlas-dbusers-create), [Atlas
  Administration API](atlas/reference/api-resources-spec/v2), Atlas UI, or
  a supported [integration](atlas/partner-integrations) to manage
  database users on Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
  any user modifications.

- The available Atlas [built-in roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) and
  [specific privileges](atlas/security-add-mongodb-users)
  support a subset of MongoDB commands. For more information, see
  [Unsupported Commands in M10+ Clusters](atlas/unsupported-commands) for
  more information.

- You can create a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas project.

sections.heading: Database User Authentication
sections.body:
Atlas offers the following forms of authentication for database
users:<Tab tabid="scram">
  Password

  [SCRAM](manual/core/security-scram) is MongoDB's
  default authentication method. SCRAM requires a password for
  each user.

  The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
  SCRAM-authenticated users is the `admin` database.

  > **Note:** By default, Atlas supports SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication.
  > If you created a user before MongoDB 4.0, you must update
  > MongoDB 4.0, update their passwords to generate SCRAM-SHA-256
  > credentials. You may reuse existing passwords.
</Tab>X.509 Certificates[X.509 Certificates](manual/core/security-x.509), also known
as mutual TLS or mTLS, allow passwordless authentication by using
a trusted certificate.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
X.509-authenticated users is the `$external` database.If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you can't connect to your
clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).AWS IAMYou can create a database user which uses an [AWS IAM](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/introduction.html)
User or Role ARN (Amazon Resource Name) for authentication.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
AWS IAM-authenticated users is the `$external` database.AWS IAM authentication is available only on clusters which use MongoDB
version 7.0 and higher.

sections.heading: Create Database Users
sections.body:
<SelectedContent>
  Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
  project.

  A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
  to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
  add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
  To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
  can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
  or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
  and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

  Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
  access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
  Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
  database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
  7-day period.

  Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
  in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
  both temporary and non-temporary database users.

  To view the project's Activity Feed:

  For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
  [Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).

  Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
  project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
  project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
  [Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).

  > **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
  > [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
  > Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
  > Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
  > any user modifications.

  # Database User Authentication

  [SCRAM](manual/core/security-scram) is MongoDB's
  default authentication method. SCRAM requires a password for
  each user.

  The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
  SCRAM-authenticated users is the `admin` database.

  > **Note:** By default, Atlas supports SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication.
  > If you have any database users created before the release of
  > MongoDB 4.0, update their passwords to generate SCRAM-SHA-256
  > credentials. You may reuse existing passwords.

  # Required Access

  To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
  [Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
  [Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.

  # Add Database Users

  Select an authentication mechanism and follow the steps to create
  a new database user using the Atlas UI.

  # Step 1: Open the  dialog box.

  - If it isn't already displayed, click the
    Database Users tab.

  - Click  Add New Database User.

  # Step 2: Select .

  In the Authentication Method section of the Add
  New Database User modal window, select the box labeled Password.

  # Step 3: Enter user information.

  Under Password Authentication, there are two text fields.

  - Enter a username for the new user in the top text field.

  - Enter a password for the new user in the lower text field.

  To use a password auto-generated by Atlas,
  click the Autogenerate Secure Password button.

  # Step 4: Assign privileges.

  Select the database user privileges. You can assign privileges to the new user
  in one or more of the following ways:

  - Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
    Built-in Role dropdown menu. You can select one
    built-in role per database user within the Atlas UI. If you delete the
    default option, you can click Add Built-in Role to select a new built-in role.

  - If you have any [custom roles](mongodb-roles) defined, you can expand
    the Custom Roles section and select
    one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu. Click
    Add Custom Role to add more custom roles. You can also
    click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
    roles for your project.

  - Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
    [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
    Specific Privileges dropdown menu. Click
    Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns the
    user specific privileges on individual databases and collections.

  Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
  privileges to a single database user.

  To remove an applied role or privilege, click 
  Delete next to the role or privilege you wish to delete.

  > **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
  > next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
  > Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
  > can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.

  For more information on authorization, see [Role-Based
  Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
  Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.

  # Step 5: Specify the resources in the project that the user can access.

  By default, users can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
  project. You can restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances
  by doing the following:

  - Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
    Database Instances to ON.

  - Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the user access to
    from the Grant Access To list.

  # Step 6: Save as temporary user.

  Toggle Temporary User to On and choose
  a time after which Atlas can delete the user from the
  Temporary User Duration dropdown. You can select one of the
  following time periods for the user to exist:

  - 6 hours

  - 1 day

  - 1 week

  In the Database Users tab, temporary users display
  the time remaining until Atlas will delete the user. Once
  Atlas deletes the user, any client or application that uses
  the temporary user's credentials loses access to the cluster.

  # Step 7: Click .

  # View Database Users and Certificates

  <DefinitionListItem>
    To view 

    Atlas

     database users and X.509 certificates in the

    Atlas UI:
  </DefinitionListItem>

  ## Step 1: View  database users.

  - If it's not already displayed, click the
    Database Users tab.

  - Click  Edit for the user
    to view their privileges, authentication details, and
    X.509 certificates.

  # Modify Database Users

  To modify existing users for an Atlas project:

  # Step 1: Modify an existing user.

  - If it's not already displayed, click the
    Database Users tab.

  - Click  Edit for the user you
    want to modify.

    You can modify the privileges and authentication details
    assigned to the user. You cannot modify the authentication
    method.

    The following table describes what you can do for each user:

    | User Type                             | Action                                                                                                                                |
    | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
    | SCRAM authenticated users             | Edit a user's password.                                                                                                               |
    | X.509 certificate authenticated users | Download a new certificate.                                                                                                           |
    | AWS IAM users                         | Modify database access privileges.                                                                                                    |
    | Temporary users                       | Modify the time period the user exists or make the user a permanent user, provided the user's expiration date has not already passed. |

    > **Note:** You cannot change a permanent user into a temporary user.
    > If you change a temporary user into a permanent user, you
    > cannot make it temporary again.

  - Click Update User to save the changes.

  # Delete Database Users

  To delete existing users for an Atlas project using the Atlas UI:
</SelectedContent>Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.To view the project's Activity Feed:For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.[X.509 Certificates](manual/core/security-x.509), also known
as mutual TLS or mTLS, allow passwordless authentication by using
a trusted certificate.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
X.509-authenticated users is the `$external` database.If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you can't connect to your
clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.Select an authentication mechanism and follow the steps to create
a new database user using the Atlas UI.* If it isn't already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

* Click  Add New Database User.In the Authentication Method section of the Add
New Database User modal window, select the box marked Certificate.* Enter a username for the new user in the Common Name text
  field.

* (Optional) If you would like to download an Atlas-managed certificate
  after the new user is created, toggle the Download certificate
  when user is added switch to On.

  > **Note:** To download the certificate upon saving, you must provide a
  > certificate expiration date.

* (Optional) Select a certificate expiration period.

  X.509 certificates expire and are invalid after the
  certificate expiration date you set. A user cannot
  log in with an expired X.509 certificate and must be issued
  a new certificate.

  To help manage this, Atlas automatically creates a
  project-level alert when you create a new user with X.509
  authentication enabled. This alert sends a notification 30 days
  before that user's latest certificate expires, repeating every
  24 hours. You can view and edit this alert from Atlas's
  Alert Settings page. For more information on
  configuring alerts, see
  Configure Alert Settings.

  > **Important:** If a user loses their certificate, they will need a new
  > certificate before they can log in again.

  > **Important:** You cannot revoke X.509 certificates. To revoke an X.509
  > certificate-authenticated user's access to your project,
  > you must delete that user.

  If you prefer to manage your own X.509 certificates, you
  can upload a PEM-encoded certificate authority through
  [self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).Select the database user privileges. You can assign privileges to the new user
in one or more of the following ways:* Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Built-in Role dropdown menu. You can select one
  built-in role per database user within the Atlas UI. If you delete the
  default option, you can click Add Built-in Role to select a new built-in role.

* If you have any [custom roles](mongodb-roles) defined, you can expand
  the Custom Roles section and select
  one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu. Click
  Add Custom Role to add more custom roles. You can also
  click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
  roles for your project.

* Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
  [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Specific Privileges dropdown menu. Click
  Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns the
  user specific privileges on individual databases and collections.Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
privileges to a single database user.To remove an applied role or privilege, click 
Delete next to the role or privilege you wish to delete.> **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
> next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
> Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
> can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.For more information on authorization, see [Role-Based
Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.Specify the resources in the project that the user can access.
By default, users can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
project. You can restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances
by doing the following:* Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
  Database Instances to ON.

* Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the user access to
  from the Grant Access To list.To view Atlas database users and X.509 certificates in theAtlas UI:

  method.

> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.You can create a database user that uses [OIDC](oidc-authentication-authorization)
for authentication.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
OIDC (OpenID Connect)-authenticated users is the `$external` database.OIDC (OpenID Connect) authentication is available only on clusters that use MongoDB
version 7.0 and higher.To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.Select an authentication mechanism and follow the steps to create
a new database user using the Atlas UI.Click  Add New Database User or Group.> **Note:** Until you [Atlas Workforce IdP configuration](apply-oidc-idp), this button says 
> Add New Database User.In the Authentication Method section, select Federated Auth.> **Note:** Until you [enable Workload IdP for your organization](configure-oidc),
> you can't select this box.a. In the Select Identity Provider section, select a configured
OIDC (OpenID Connect) Identity Provider.- Specify either the user identifier or group identifier associated with
  your configured Workforce Identity Provider.> **Note:** For Azure Entra ID users, this value maps to the Object Id of your
> Azure user group rather than user group name.To assign privileges to the new user or group, do one or more of the
following tasks:* Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Built-in Role dropdown menu.

  - You can select one built-in role per database group in the Atlas UI.

  - If you delete the default option, you can click Add Built-in Role
    to select a new built-in role.

* Select or add [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

  - If you have any custom roles defined, you can expand
    the Custom Roles section and select
    one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu.

  - Click Add Custom Role to add more custom roles.

  - Click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
    roles for your project.

* Add [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles).

  - Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
    privileges from the Specific Privileges dropdown menu.

  - Click Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns
    the group specific privileges on individual databases and collections.

* Remove an applied role or privilege.

  - Click 

     

    Delete

     next to the

    role or privilege to delete.

  > **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
  > next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
  > Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
  > can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
privileges to a database group.To learn more about authorization, see [Role-Based
Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.By default, groups can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
project. To restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances:* Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
  Database Instances to On.

* Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the group access to
  from the Grant Access To list.Toggle Temporary User or Temporary Group to
On and choose a time after which Atlas can delete the user
or group from the Temporary User Duration or
Temporary Group Duration dropdown. You can select one of the
following time periods for the group to exist:* 6 hours

* 1 day

* 1 weekIn the Database Users tab, temporary users or groups display
the time remaining until Atlas deletes the users or group. After
Atlas deletes the user or group, any client or application that uses
the temporary user's or group's credentials loses access to the cluster.Do one of the following steps:* If you added a user, click the Add User button.

* If you added a group, click the Add Group button.To view Atlas database users and X.509 certificates in theAtlas UI:

project.A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.To view the project's Activity Feed:For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.You can create a database user which uses an [AWS IAM](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/introduction.html)
User or Role ARN (Amazon Resource Name) for authentication.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
AWS IAM-authenticated users is the `$external` database.AWS IAM authentication is available only on clusters which use MongoDB
version 7.0 and higher.To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.Select an authentication mechanism and follow the steps to create
a new database user using the Atlas UI.* If it isn't already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

* Click  Add New Database User.In the Authentication Method section of the Add
New Database User modal window, select the box marked AWS IAM.* Select a user type from the AWS IAM Type dropdown menu.

* Enter an AWS user ARN (Amazon Resource Name). Click the
  See instruction below link for help with finding your ARN.Select the database user privileges. You can assign privileges to the new user
in one or more of the following ways:* Select a [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Built-in Role dropdown menu. You can select one
  built-in role per database user within the Atlas UI. If you delete the
  default option, you can click Add Built-in Role to select a new built-in role.

* If you have any [custom roles](mongodb-roles) defined, you can expand
  the Custom Roles section and select
  one or more roles from the Custom Roles dropdown menu. Click
  Add Custom Role to add more custom roles. You can also
  click the Custom Roles link to see the custom
  roles for your project.

* Expand the Specific Privileges section and select one or more
  [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles) from the
  Specific Privileges dropdown menu. Click
  Add Specific Privilege to add more privileges. This assigns the
  user specific privileges on individual databases and collections.Atlas can apply a built-in role, multiple custom roles, and multiple specific
privileges to a single database user.To remove an applied role or privilege, click 
Delete next to the role or privilege you wish to delete.> **Note:** Atlas doesn't display the  Delete icon
> next to your Built-in Role, Custom Role, or
> Specific Privilege selection if you selected only one option. You
> can delete the selected role or privilege once you apply another role or privilege.For more information on authorization, see [Role-Based
Access Control](manual/core/authorization) and [Built-in
Roles](manual/reference/built-in-roles) in the MongoDB manual.By default, users can access all the clusters and federated database instances in the
project. You can restrict access to specific clusters and federated database instances
by doing the following:* Toggle Restrict Access to Specific Clusters/Federated
  Database Instances to ON.

* Select the clusters and federated database instances to grant the user access to
  from the Grant Access To list.Toggle Temporary User to On and choose
a time after which Atlas can delete the user from the
Temporary User Duration dropdown. You can select one of the
following time periods for the user to exist:* 6 hours

* 1 day

* 1 weekIn the Database Users tab, temporary users display
the time remaining until Atlas will delete the user. Once
Atlas deletes the user, any client or application that uses
the temporary user's credentials loses access to the cluster.To view Atlas database users and X.509 certificates in theAtlas UI:

sections.heading: Step 1: View  database users.
sections.body:
- If it's not already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Edit for the user
  to view their privileges, authentication details, and
  X.509 certificates.To modify existing users for an Atlas project:- If it's not already displayed, click the
  Database Users tab.

- Click  Edit for the user you
  want to modify.

  You can modify the privileges and authentication details
  assigned to the user. You cannot modify the authentication
  method.

  The following table describes what you can do for each user:

  | User Type                             | Action                                                                                                                                |
  | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | SCRAM authenticated users             | Edit a user's password.                                                                                                               |
  | X.509 certificate authenticated users | Download a new certificate.                                                                                                           |
  | AWS IAM users                         | Modify database access privileges.                                                                                                    |
  | Temporary users                       | Modify the time period the user exists or make the user a permanent user, provided the user's expiration date has not already passed. |

  > **Note:** You cannot change a permanent user into a temporary user.
  > If you change a temporary user into a permanent user, you
  > cannot make it temporary again.

- Click Update User to save the changes.To delete existing users for an Atlas project using the Atlas UI:Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.To view the project's Activity Feed:For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.[SCRAM](manual/core/security-scram) is MongoDB's
default authentication method. SCRAM requires a password for
each user.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
SCRAM-authenticated users is the `admin` database.> **Note:** By default, Atlas supports SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication.
> If you have any database users created before the release of
> MongoDB 4.0, update their passwords to generate SCRAM-SHA-256
> credentials. You may reuse existing passwords.To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.The Atlas CLI uses the following commands to create new database users and X.509 certificates. The options you specify determine the authentication method.To create a database user for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers create [builtInRole]... [options]

```To create a new Atlas-managed X.509 certificate for the specified database user using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers certs create [options]

```To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create) and
[atlas dbusers certs create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-create).Related Links* [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

* [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

sections.heading: View Database Users
sections.body:
To list all Atlas database users for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers list [options]

```To return the details for a single Atlas database user in the project you specify using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers describe <username> [options]

```To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-list) and
[atlas dbusers describe](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-describe).Related Links* [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

* [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

sections.heading: View X.509 Certificates for a Database User
sections.body:
To list all Atlas-managed, unexpired certificates for a database user using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers certs list <username> [options]

```To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers certs list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-list).To update a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers update <username> [options]

```To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers update](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-update).To delete a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers delete <username> [options]

```To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-delete).Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.To view the project's Activity Feed:For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.Atlas offers the following forms of authentication for database users:> **Note:** Starting with MongoDB 8.0, LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and authorization is
> deprecated. The feature is available and will continue to operate
> without changes throughout the lifetime of MongoDB 8. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) will be
> removed in a future major release.
>
> For details, see [LDAP Deprecation](manual/core/LDAP-deprecation).You can create a database user that uses [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization)
for authentication.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)-authenticated users is the `$external` database.If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you
can't connect to your clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.The Atlas CLI uses the following commands to create new database users and X.509 certificates. The options you specify determine the authentication method.To create a database user for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers create [builtInRole]... [options]

```To create a new Atlas-managed X.509 certificate for the specified database user using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers certs create [options]

```To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create) and
[atlas dbusers certs create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-create).Related Links* [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

* [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have

Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary

7-day period.Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users

project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),

> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.[X.509 Certificates](manual/core/security-x.509), also known
as mutual TLS or mTLS, allow passwordless authentication by using
a trusted certificate.The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
X.509-authenticated users is the `$external` database.If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you can't connect to your
clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.The Atlas CLI uses the following commands to create new database users and X.509 certificates. The options you specify determine the authentication method.To create a database user for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers create [builtInRole]... [options]

```To create a new Atlas-managed X.509 certificate for the specified database user using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers certs create [options]

```To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create) and
[atlas dbusers certs create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-create).Related Links* [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

* [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

version 7.0 and higher.To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.The Atlas CLI uses the following commands to create new database users and X.509 certificates. The options you specify determine the authentication method.To create a database user for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers create [builtInRole]... [options]

```To create a new Atlas-managed X.509 certificate for the specified database user using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers certs create [options]

```To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create) and
[atlas dbusers certs create](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-create).Related Links* [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

* [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

sections.heading: View Database Users
sections.body:
To list all Atlas database users for your project using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers list [options]

```To return the details for a single Atlas database user in the project you specify using the Atlas CLI, run the following command:```sh

atlas dbusers describe <username> [options]

```To learn more about the syntax and parameters for the previous commands, see the Atlas CLI documentation for
[atlas dbusers list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-list) and
[atlas dbusers describe](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-describe).Related Links* [Install the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/install-atlas-cli)

* [Connect to the Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/connect-atlas-cli)

sections.heading: View X.509 Certificates for a Database User
sections.body:
To list all Atlas-managed, unexpired certificates for a database user using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers certs list <username> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers certs list](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-certs-list).

To update a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers update <username> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers update](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-update).

To delete a database user from your project using the
Atlas CLI, run the following command:

```sh

atlas dbusers delete <username> [options]

```

To learn more about the command syntax and parameters, see the
Atlas CLI documentation for [atlas dbusers delete](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-delete).

Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.

A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.

Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.

To view the project's Activity Feed:

For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).

Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).

> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.

[SCRAM](manual/core/security-scram) is MongoDB's
default authentication method. SCRAM requires a password for
each user.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
SCRAM-authenticated users is the `admin` database.

> **Note:** By default, Atlas supports SCRAM-SHA-256 authentication.
> If you have any database users created before the release of
> MongoDB 4.0, update their passwords to generate SCRAM-SHA-256
> credentials. You may reuse existing passwords.

To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.

You can add database users through the Atlas Administration API. The
options you specify determine the authentication method. To learn
more, see [Create One Database User](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupdatabaseuser).

To view Atlas database users using the
Atlas Administration API, see [Get All](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupdatabaseusers).

You can update database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Update One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupdatabaseuser).

You can delete database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Delete One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-deletegroupdatabaseuser).

Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.

A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.

Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.

To view the project's Activity Feed:

For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).

Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).

> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.

[X.509 Certificates](manual/core/security-x.509), also known
as mutual TLS or mTLS, allow passwordless authentication by using
a trusted certificate.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
X.509-authenticated users is the `$external` database.

If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you can't connect to your
clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).

To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.

You can add database users through the Atlas Administration API. The
options you specify determine the authentication method. To learn
more, see [Create One Database User](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupdatabaseuser).

To view Atlas database users using the
Atlas Administration API, see [Get All](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupdatabaseusers).

You can update database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Update One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupdatabaseuser).

You can delete database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Delete One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-deletegroupdatabaseuser).

Create database users to provide clients access to the clusters in your
project.

A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned
to the user. When you create a database user, any of the [built-in role](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)
and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

Database users are separate from Atlas users. Database users have
access to MongoDB databases, while Atlas users have access to the
Atlas application itself. Atlas supports creating temporary
database users that automatically expire within a user-configurable
7-day period.

Atlas audits the creation, deletion, and updates of database users
in the project's Activity Feed. Atlas audits actions pertaining to
both temporary and non-temporary database users.

To view the project's Activity Feed:

For more information on the project Activity Feed, see
[Activity Feed](project-activity-feed).

Atlas supports a maximum of 100 database users per Atlas
project by default. If you require more than 100 database users on a
project, you can use the [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-setgrouplimit) to increase the limit. For assistance, contact
[Atlas support](https://cloud.mongodb.com/support).

> **Important:** You must use the [Atlas CLI](atlas/cli/current/command/atlas-dbusers-create),
> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.

> **Note:** Starting with MongoDB 8.0, LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication and authorization is
> deprecated. The feature is available and will continue to operate
> without changes throughout the lifetime of MongoDB 8. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) will be
> removed in a future major release.
>
> For details, see [LDAP Deprecation](manual/core/LDAP-deprecation).

You can create a database user that uses [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization)
for authentication.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)-authenticated users is the `$external` database.

If you [LDAP authentication and authorization](ldaps-authentication-authorization), you
can't connect to your clusters with users that authenticate with an
Atlas-managed X.509 certificate. To enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) and
connecting to your clusters with X.509 users, see
[self-managed X.509 certificate](self-managed-x509).

To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.

You can add database users through the Atlas Administration API. The
options you specify determine the authentication method. To learn
more, see [Create One Database User](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupdatabaseuser).

To view Atlas database users using the
Atlas Administration API, see [Get All](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupdatabaseusers).

You can update database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Update One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupdatabaseuser).

You can delete database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Delete One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-deletegroupdatabaseuser).

project.

add the user to all clusters in your Atlas project.
To specify which resources a database user can access in your project, you
can select the option Restrict Access to Specific Clusters in the Atlas UI
or set [privileges](manual/reference/built-in-roles)

> [Atlas Administration API](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/group/endpoint-database-users),
> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.

You can create a database user that uses [OIDC](oidc-authentication-authorization)
for authentication.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
OIDC (OpenID Connect)-authenticated users is the `$external` database.

OIDC (OpenID Connect) authentication is available only on clusters that use MongoDB
version 7.0 and higher.

To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.

You can add database users through the Atlas Administration API. The
options you specify determine the authentication method. To learn
more, see [Create One Database User](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupdatabaseuser).

Atlas Administration API, see [Get All](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupdatabaseusers).

learn more, see [Update One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupdatabaseuser).

You can delete database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Delete One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-deletegroupdatabaseuser).

project.

A database user's access is determined by the roles assigned

and [custom roles](mongodb-roles).

> Atlas UI, or a supported [integration](partner-integrations) to add, modify, or delete database users on
> Atlas clusters. Otherwise, Atlas rolls back
> any user modifications.

You can create a database user which uses an [AWS IAM](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide/introduction.html)
User or Role ARN (Amazon Resource Name) for authentication.

The [authentication database](manual/core/security-users) for
AWS IAM-authenticated users is the `$external` database.

AWS IAM authentication is available only on clusters which use MongoDB
version 7.0 and higher.

To add database users, you must have [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>),
[Organization Stream Processing Admin](<Organization Stream Processing Admin>), [Project Owner](<Project Owner>), [Project Stream Processing Owner](<Project Stream Processing Owner>), or
[Project Database Access Admin](<Project Database Access Admin>) access to Atlas.

You can add database users through the Atlas Administration API. The
options you specify determine the authentication method. To learn
more, see [Create One Database User](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-creategroupdatabaseuser).

To view Atlas database users using the
Atlas Administration API, see [Get All](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-listgroupdatabaseusers).

You can update database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Update One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-updategroupdatabaseuser).

You can delete database users through the Atlas Administration API. To
learn more, see [Delete One](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-deletegroupdatabaseuser).
